name: Fetal glucose utilisation per kg
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 textbook 'An Introduction to Fetal Physiology': the canonical reference for fetal substrate utilisation rates derived from chronically-catheterised lamb studies. Human extrapolation by body-mass scaling.

source_check.source_citation: Simultaneous umbilical uptake / fetal glucose utilization in sheep (PMID 7246734)
source_check.extracted_value: Fetal glucose utilization 5.58 mg/min/kg ([14C]) in fed normoglycemic sheep; umbilical uptake 4.77 mg/min/kg.

notes: Net glucose utilisation by the late-gestation human fetus per kilogram body weight.

name: Fetal oxygen consumption per kg
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 textbook: fetal oxygen consumption ~7 mL/kg/min from lamb studies, well-replicated in subsequent ovine work; human estimate by body-mass scaling.

source_check.source_citation: Acute maternal hypoxia / fetal oxygenation in sheep (PMID 6767635)
source_check.quote: The mean oxygen consumption was 8.4 +/- 1.9 ml/min/kg in the near-term fetal sheep.
source_check.extracted_value: Mean near-term fetal sheep oxygen consumption is 8.4 +/- 1.9 mL/min/kg.

notes: Whole-body oxygen consumption of the late-gestation human fetus per kilogram body weight.

name: Fetal haemoglobin (HbF) P50 at term
tier_rationale: Tier A from Bauer, Ludwig & Ludwig 1969 Respiration Physiology (PMID 4980905, DOI 10.1016/0034-5687(69)90010-3), the foundational study comparing adult vs fetal haemoglobin O2 affinity. Fetal HbF P50 ~19-20 mmHg is much lower than adult HbA (~26.6), reflecting HbF's reduced affinity for 2,3-DPG. The left-shift is the key adaptation enabling fetal blood to extract O2 from low-PO2 placental blood. Triangulated across every standard hematology and neonatal-physiology reference.

extraction.method: triangulated from Bauer 1969 + standard hematology references

notes: The 7 mmHg leftward P50 shift relative to adult HbA is the key biophysical adaptation enabling fetal-side O2 extraction.

name: Fetal haemoglobin concentration at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from standard neonatal-haematology references: cord-blood haemoglobin at term ~17 g/dL — significantly higher than maternal (~11.5 g/dL). The high Hb concentration partially compensates for the low umbilical-vein PO2: fetal arterial O2 content is comparable to adult despite much lower PO2. Hytten & Chamberlain 1980 covers fetal haematology; modern neonatal guidelines confirm.
notes: Higher fetal Hb + leftward P50 together close most of the gap between fetal-side PO2 and adult-side PO2 in arterial O2 content.

name: Umbilical artery pH at term (cord blood)
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 and standard neonatal practice: term umbilical-artery pH (deoxygenated fetal blood returning to the placenta) is typically 7.20-7.34 with mean ~7.27. Values <7.10 trigger clinical concern; <7.00 with elevated base deficit defines significant acidaemia. The arterial-venous pH gradient (~0.07) reflects net acid load from fetal tissue metabolism.

source_check.quote: Studies showed that in term infants with uncomplicated delivery, the mean cord arterial pH is 7.24 to 7.27, and the mean cord venous pH 7.32 to 7.34.
source_check.extracted_value: Mean umbilical arterial pH in uncomplicated term deliveries is 7.24 to 7.27.

applicability.population: Healthy term newborn, cord blood, uncomplicated vaginal delivery

notes: Cord-blood pH is the standard clinical metric for intrapartum fetal acid-base status.

name: Umbilical vein pH at term (cord blood)
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986: term umbilical-vein pH (freshly oxygenated blood returning from the placenta to the fetus) is typically 7.28-7.40 with mean ~7.34. The vein-artery pH difference (~0.07) is the principal indicator that the cord-blood sample was correctly identified — a small or absent gradient suggests the sample was drawn from the wrong vessel.

source_check.quote: Studies showed that in term infants with uncomplicated delivery, the mean cord arterial pH is 7.24 to 7.27, and the mean cord venous pH 7.32 to 7.34.
source_check.extracted_value: Mean umbilical venous pH in uncomplicated term deliveries is 7.32 to 7.34.

applicability.population: Healthy term newborn, cord blood, uncomplicated vaginal delivery

name: Umbilical artery lactate at term (cord blood)
tier_rationale: Tier C from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 and standard obstetric texts: cord-blood lactate at uncomplicated term delivery is typically 2-6 mmol/L (well above adult resting ~1 mmol/L). The high baseline reflects partial reliance on anaerobic metabolism in the fetal circulation and the burst of activity at delivery. Lactate >8 mmol/L is associated with intrapartum hypoxia. Distribution is heavily right-skewed; central value ~3.5 reflects the modal cohort mean.

source_check.source_citation: Neonatology (Karger): Reference Values for Umbilical Artery Lactate
source_check.quote: Lactate values at the 50th, 90th, and 97th percentile were 3.4, 7.0, and 9.0 mmol/L.
source_check.extracted_value: Population umbilical artery lactate median (50th percentile) is 3.4 mmol/L.

applicability.population: Healthy term newborn, cord blood, uncomplicated vaginal delivery

name: Fetal plasma glucose at term (cord blood)
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986: fetal plasma glucose is closely coupled to maternal glucose by trans-placental GLUT1/GLUT3 transport and runs at approximately 70-75% of maternal levels. Term cord values typically 2.8-4.5 mmol/L. Fetal glucose follows maternal glucose dynamically; the fetus does not contribute substantial endogenous glucose production until the perinatal switch.

source_check.source_citation: Holme et al., Placental Glucose Transfer: A Human In Vivo Study, PLOS ONE 2015
source_check.quote: The transplacental maternal-fetal glucose gradient was 1.22 (0.42) mmol/L.
source_check.extracted_value: At term: umbilical vein glucose 4.09, umbilical artery 3.71, maternal radial artery 4.71 mmol/L; gradient 1.22 mmol/L.

applicability.population: Healthy term newborn, cord blood, mother fasted ~overnight

notes: Fetal/maternal glucose ratio ~0.7 is one of the most robust quantitative facts in fetal metabolism.

name: Fetal pulmonary fluid net secretion rate, mid-gestation baseline
tier_rationale: Tier D (hypothesis-only): Strang 1991 reviews fetal lung liquid as chloride-driven secretion at ~4-5 mL/kg/h through most of gestation. Human in vivo rates are extrapolated from ovine and other animal data and are not directly measurable in human fetuses; the central value is illustrative. DO NOT USE FOR PREDICTION.

source_check.source_citation: Pfister et al., Lung liquid volume and secretion rate in fetal sheep, J Physiol 2001 (PMC2278829)
source_check.quote: In the first phase of its decline, mean secretion rate fell from 5.3 to 3.1 ml kg−1 h−1, before decreasing in the second phase to 1.8 ml kg−1 h−1.
source_check.extracted_value: Pre-labour fetal sheep lung liquid secretion rate begins ~5.3 mL/kg/h, declining toward labour.

notes: Hypothesis-only Tier D parameter used in fetal_pulmonary_fluid_trajectory submodel. Positive sign = secretion (fluid into lung lumen).

name: Fetal pulmonary fluid net rate near term (transition to reabsorption)
tier_rationale: Tier D (hypothesis-only): near term, fetal lung liquid net flux reverses from secretion to active sodium-driven reabsorption (Strang 1991); during labour reabsorption accelerates further. Quantitative human rates are species-extrapolated and the timing of the switch varies. DO NOT USE FOR PREDICTION.
notes: Hypothesis-only Tier D parameter. Negative sign = net reabsorption.

name: Fetal core temperature at term
tier_rationale: Tier B from Battaglia & Meschia 1986: fetal core temperature runs approximately 0.3-0.5 °C above maternal core temperature throughout late gestation, driven by the fetus's high mass-specific metabolic rate and the limited heat-dissipation pathway (placental blood). The maternal-fetal temperature gradient is one of the more robust quantitative facts in fetal thermal physiology.

source_check.source_citation: Asakura, Fetal and Neonatal Thermoregulation, J Nippon Med Sch 2004;71(6):360
source_check.quote: heat is transferred to the fetus via the placenta and the uterus, resulting in a 0.3C° to 0.5C° higher temperature than that of the mother
source_check.extracted_value: The fetus runs 0.3-0.5 °C warmer than the mother via placental/uterine heat transfer.

notes: Maternal hyperthermia (fever, exercise) is transmitted to the fetus with attenuated amplitude and delay; the gradient is maintained.

name: Fetal urine production rate at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Battaglia & Meschia 1986 and standard obstetric texts: fetal urine production rate at term is approximately 30-70 mL/kg/h (~600-1200 mL/day for a 3.5 kg fetus), accounting for the majority of amniotic-fluid turnover. Direct in vivo measurements rely on ultrasound bladder-volume cycling; estimates vary across cohorts and techniques.
notes: Fetal urine is the dominant source of amniotic fluid in late gestation; fetal swallowing is the dominant sink. Disorders of either side present as poly- or oligohydramnios.

name: Fetal plasma insulin at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Economides et al. 1989 Am J Obstet Gynecol (cordocentesis series in normally-grown fetuses) and corroborating fetal-endocrine studies: term fetal plasma insulin centres ~50 pmol/L (about a quarter of typical adult fasting). Wide between-individual variability reflects both glucose-stimulated dynamics at the moment of sampling and assay sensitivity in the low-pmol range.

source_check.source_citation: Cetin et al., Cord Plasma Insulin in Healthy Term Neonates, Int J Endocrinol 2013 (PMC3614788)
source_check.extracted_value: Cord plasma insulin in healthy term AGA neonates 68.4 +/- 54.6 pmol/L (LGA 82.1 +/- 56.2).

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies, third trimester, appropriate-for-gestational-age fetuses

notes: Fetal hyperinsulinaemia (well above this range) is the proximate cause of macrosomia in maternal diabetes; out of scope for nidus.

name: Fetal plasma cortisol at term
tier_rationale: Tier C from Murphy 1973 Am J Obstet Gynecol (cord-blood cortisol at delivery) and later confirmatory series: term umbilical cortisol centres ~10–20 μg/dL, having risen substantially from low mid-gestation values. The late-gestation cortisol surge is thought to contribute to fetal organ maturation (lung surfactant) and the timing of parturition. Mode of delivery (labour vs elective caesarean) is a major source of variability.

source_check.source_citation: Umbilical cord cortisol in breech vs vertex delivery (PMID 3973861, via Europe PMC)
source_check.quote: The mean umbilical cord total cortisol concentration was 790 +/- 363 nmol/liter in breech delivery as compared with 493 +/- 125 nmol/liter in vertex delivery.
source_check.extracted_value: Mean umbilical cord total cortisol in vertex delivery 493 +/- 125 nmol/L (~17.9 ug/dL); breech 790 +/- 363 nmol/L.

applicability.population: Healthy singleton pregnancies at term; cord-blood sampling at delivery

notes: Cord cortisol is higher after labour than after elective caesarean — a stress effect rather than baseline difference.
